name: Causal set theory
summary: Postulates that spacetime at the fundamental level is a discrete partial order — a 'causal set' — in which each element is an event and the partial order encodes causal relations. The continuum spacetime of GR emerges as an approximation in the large-N limit. Provides a Lorentz-invariant discreteness (no preferred frame) and gives an heuristic explanation for the small cosmological constant via 'fluctuating Lambda' arguments.

addresses_puzzles.note: Sorkin's prediction of a fluctuating Lambda ~ 1/sqrt(N) where N is the spacetime volume in fundamental units gives Lambda ~ H_0^2 today, in remarkable agreement with the observed value. This is one of the most quantitative 'predictions' of any quantum-gravity approach for Lambda.

touches_benchmarks.note: Discrete CST preserves Lorentz invariance because the discreteness is set by Poisson sprinkling, which is invariant in distribution under Lorentz transformations. This distinguishes CST from many other discrete-spacetime approaches.

introduces.note: Discreteness scale (typically Planck-scale); the dynamics requires choosing a sum-over-causal-sets prescription.

history: Bombelli, Lee, Meyer, Sorkin (1987), 'Space-time as a causal set', Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 521. Sorkin's everpresent-Lambda prediction (1990s-2000s) gives Lambda ~ 1/sqrt(N) ~ H_0^2 with no free parameter at the order-of-magnitude level. Active small-community research with periodic phenomenological proposals.

references.citation: Bombelli, Lee, Meyer, Sorkin, 'Space-time as a causal set', Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 521 (1987)
Surya, 'The causal set approach to quantum gravity', Living Reviews in Relativity 22, 5 (2019)
